snp4.12:2.3: Which of these statements is true
snp4.12:2.4: when all of them say they are skilled?”
snp4.12:3.1: “If, in not accepting an opponent’s doctrine,
snp4.12:3.2: one’s a fool, a beast of inferior discernment,
snp4.12:3.3: then all are fools of inferior discernment—
snp4.12:3.4: all of these who dwell on their views.
snp4.12:4.1: But if, in siding with a view, one’s cleansed,
snp4.12:4.2: with discernment made pure, sensible, skilled,
snp4.12:4.3: then none of them are of inferior discernment,
snp4.12:4.4: for all of them have their own views.
snp4.12:5.1:
I don’t say,
‘That’s how it is,’
snp4.12:5.2: the way fools tell one another.
snp4.12:5.3: They each make out their views to be true
snp4.12:5.4: and so regard their opponents as fools.”
snp4.12:6.1: “What some say is true —’That’s how it is’—
snp4.12:6.4: Why can’t contemplatives say one thing & the same?”
snp4.12:7.1: “The truth is one, there is no second
snp4.12:7.2: about which a person who knows it would argue with one who knows.
snp4.12:7.3: Contemplatives promote their various own truths,
snp4.12:7.4: that’s why they don’t say one thing & the same.”
snp4.12:8.1: “But why do they say various truths,
snp4.12:8.2: those who say they are skilled?
snp4.12:8.3: Have they learned many various truths
snp4.12:8.4:
or do they follow conjecture?”
“Apart from their perception
snp4.12:9.3: Theorizing conjectures with regard to views,
snp4.12:9.4:
they speak of a pair:
true & false.
snp4.12:10.1: Dependent on what’s seen, heard, & sensed, dependent on habits & practices,
snp4.12:10.3: Taking a stance on his decisions, praising himself, he says,
snp4.12:11.1: That by which he regards his opponents as fools
snp4.12:11.2: is that by which he says he is skilled.
snp4.12:11.4: he despises another who speaks the same way.
snp4.12:12.1: Agreeing on a view gone out of bounds,
snp4.12:12.2: drunk with conceit, imagining himself perfect,
snp4.12:12.3: he has consecrated, with his own mind, himself
snp4.12:13.1: If, by an opponent’s word, one’s inferior,
snp4.12:13.2: the opponent’s of inferior discernment as well.
snp4.12:13.3: But if, by one’s own word one’s an attainer-of-knowledge, enlightened,
snp4.12:13.4: no one among contemplatives is a fool.
snp4.12:14.1: ‘Those who approve of a doctrine other than this
snp4.12:14.3: That’s what the many sectarians say,
snp4.12:14.4: for they’re smitten with passion for their own views.
snp4.12:15.1: ‘Only here is there purity,’ that’s what they say.
snp4.12:15.2: ‘In no other doctrine is purity,’ they say.
snp4.12:15.3: That’s how the many sectarians are entrenched,
snp4.12:15.4: speaking firmly there concerning their own path.
snp4.12:16.1: Speaking firmly concerning your own path,
snp4.12:16.2: what opponent here would you take as a fool?
snp4.12:16.3: You’d simply bring strife on yourself
snp4.12:16.4: if you said your opponent’s a fool with an impure doctrine.
snp4.12:17.1: Taking a stance on your decisions, & yourself as your measure,
snp4.12:17.2: you dispute further down into the world.
snp4.12:17.3: But a person who’s abandoned all decisions
snp4.12:17.4: creates no strife in the world.”
